content.assessment: From an implementation-planning lens, status quo requires no new actor capability or sequencing, making it trivially deliverable, but the lens's own criteria (monitoring feeding adaptation, failure containment) are demonstrably unmet: existing monitoring infrastructure has not historically produced correction. This is a case where 'can actors deliver the mechanism' is answered yes, but the mechanism itself contains no adaptive loop, so deliverability is not evidence of adequacy.
opportunities: Could be paired with an explicit trigger-based commitment (e.g., pre-specified segregation thresholds that mandate review) to convert passive monitoring into an adaptive loop
Existing data infrastructure could be repurposed as a baseline for eventually piloting alternatives (T6) without additional data-system investment
strengths: No new actor capacity, sequencing, or coordination is required since the mechanism is already operating (F-live-implementation_planning-04)
Assessment infrastructure (National ABC, PISA) is already institutionalized and does not require new monitoring systems to be built (F-live-implementation_planning-04)
threats: Ministerial acknowledgment of problems (F-live-implementation_planning-05) without mechanism change may accelerate legitimacy erosion, undermining future implementation capacity for any reform by increasing public distrust in state follow-through

Structural teacher shortage and thin workforce capacity mean that even if reform becomes necessary later, foundational capacity will not have been built during the monitoring period (F-live-implementation_planning-07)
weaknesses: Monitoring exists but has no demonstrated adaptation pathway — PISA and ABC data have not historically translated into structural policy correction (F-live-implementation_planning-04)

Centralized governance structure (KLIK) has created bottlenecks rather than facilitating responsive policy adjustment, undermining any future ability to act on monitoring signals (F-live-implementation_planning-03)
